bytes.topic: Routing — defining routes, route parameters, grouping and prefixing
bytes.title: Phoenix Routing — Scopes, Parameters, and Pipeline Grouping
bytes.body:
Phoenix's router lives in `router.ex` and uses a declarative DSL built on `Plug.Router`. Every route is tied to a pipeline — a composable chain of plugs that pre-process requests.

Define basic routes with `get`, `post`, `put`, `delete`, or `resources` for RESTful CRUD. Route parameters use the `:param` syntax and land in `conn.params`:

```elixir
get "/users/:id", UserController, :show
```

Group related routes under a `scope` to share a path prefix and optionally an alias:

```elixir
scope "/api/v1", MyAppWeb do
  pipe_through :api
  resources "/users", UserController, only: [:index, :show, :create]
  get "/users/:id/posts", PostController, :user_posts
end
```

`pipe_through :api` applies the `:api` pipeline (typically JSON parsing, auth plugs). Nest scopes for versioning — an outer `/api` scope with inner `/v1` and `/v2` children. Use `resources` with `:only` or `:except` to avoid generating unused routes. Run `mix phx.routes` to inspect every registered path, verb, and controller action in one shot.
bytes.code_snippet:
# router.ex
pipeline :api do
  plug :accepts, ["json"]
  plug MyAppWeb.Auth.VerifyToken
end

scope "/api", MyAppWeb do
  scope "/v1" do
    pipe_through :api

    resources "/users", UserController, only: [:index, :show, :create, :delete]
    get "/users/:id/feed", FeedController, :user_feed
    post "/users/:id/follow", FollowController, :create
  end
end

bytes.topic: Middleware — request pipeline, global vs route-specific, ordering matters
bytes.title: Phoenix Plugs — Request Pipeline, Global vs Route-Specific, and Why Order Is Everything
bytes.body:
Phoenix's middleware system is built entirely on Plugs — composable modules that transform the `%Conn{}` struct. Every request flows through your Endpoint first (global plugs: `Plug.RequestId`, `Plug.Telemetry`, parsers, session), then enters the Router where you control pipeline composition per route group.

Define pipelines with `pipeline/2`, apply them with `pipe_through/1`. Order within a pipeline is strictly top-to-bottom. A plug calling `halt/1` on `conn` short-circuits every plug that follows — which is exactly how auth guards work: fail fast before hitting business logic.

Pipelines stack: a route inside a scoped `pipe_through [:api, :require_admin]` runs both pipelines sequentially. This is more composable than global middleware registration in frameworks like Express or Django — you opt routes into behavior rather than opting out.

Put cross-cutting concerns (telemetry, request IDs, static files) in `endpoint.ex`. Put domain-specific concerns (auth, rate limiting, locale) in named router pipelines. Never duplicate a plug across multiple pipelines when a shared pipeline serves the same routes.
bytes.code_snippet:
pipeline :api do
  plug :accepts, ["json"]
  plug MyApp.Plugs.RateLimiter
  plug MyApp.Plugs.RequireAuth
end

pipeline :require_admin do
  plug MyApp.Plugs.RequireAdmin
end

scope "/api", MyAppWeb do
  pipe_through :api

  resources "/posts", PostController

  scope "/admin" do
    pipe_through [:require_admin]
    resources "/users", UserController
  end
end

bytes.topic: Project Structure — MVC vs vertical slice, feature folders, layering conventions
bytes.title: Phoenix Project Structure — Contexts Are Your Vertical Slices
bytes.body: Phoenix's generator enforces a two-root split: `lib/my_app/` for domain logic and `lib/my_app_web/` for HTTP concerns. This isn't MVC in the Rails sense — it's a deliberate boundary. Contexts (`Accounts`, `Billing`, `Catalog`) are Phoenix's idiomatic vertical slice unit. Each context owns its schemas, changesets, and query functions. The web layer calls only context public APIs — controllers and LiveViews must never reach into `Accounts.User` directly; they call `Accounts.get_user!/1`. Phoenix 1.7+ reinforces this in the web layer too: `lib/my_app_web/live/` feature folders colocate a LiveView with its components (e.g., `user_registration_live/`, `user_registration_live/form_component.ex`). For growing apps, nest within a context: `accounts/user.ex`, `accounts/auth.ex`, `accounts/token.ex` — the context module stays the public facade. Resist the one-giant-context trap; `mix phx.gen.context` gives you the scaffolding, but boundary discipline — no cross-context schema imports — is enforced only by convention.
bytes.code_snippet:
# lib/my_app/accounts.ex — public API facade
defmodule MyApp.Accounts do
  alias MyApp.Accounts.{User, Auth}
  alias MyApp.Repo

  def get_user!(id), do: Repo.get!(User, id)
  def authenticate(email, password), do: Auth.verify(email, password)
end

# lib/my_app_web/live/user_login_live.ex
defmodule MyAppWeb.UserLoginLive do
  use MyAppWeb, :live_view
  alias MyApp.Accounts  # ✓ context only — never alias Accounts.User here
end

bytes.topic: Input Validation — schema validation at the boundary, sanitization, error responses
bytes.title: Phoenix Input Validation — Ecto Changesets at the Controller Boundary
bytes.body: Phoenix's answer to boundary validation isn't middleware — it's Ecto changesets applied to embedded schemas directly in your controller or context. Define an embedded schema with typed fields, pipe params through `cast/3`, layer on `validate_required/2`, `validate_length/3`, `validate_format/3`, and reject invalid input before it touches your business logic. This gives you compile-time field awareness, automatic type coercion (string "42" → integer 42), and a structured error map that Phoenix renders cleanly via `Ecto.Changeset.traverse_errors/2`. Wire a `FallbackController` with `action_fallback MyAppWeb.FallbackController` and pattern-match on `{:error, %Ecto.Changeset{}}` to return consistent 422 JSON responses across every endpoint. Crucially, never trust `conn.params` directly — always `cast` against an explicit field allowlist. Fields not listed in `cast/3`'s third argument are silently dropped, giving you mass-assignment protection for free. For sanitization, run string fields through `String.trim/1` before casting or add a custom `validate_change` that rejects known injection patterns.
bytes.code_snippet:
defmodule MyAppWeb.UserController do
  use MyAppWeb, :controller
  action_fallback MyAppWeb.FallbackController

  @types %{name: :string, age: :integer, email: :string}

  defp validate_params(params) do
    {%{}, @types}
    |> Ecto.Changeset.cast(params, Map.keys(@types))
    |> Ecto.Changeset.validate_required([:name, :email])
    |> Ecto.Changeset.validate_length(:name, min: 2, max: 50)
    |> Ecto.Changeset.validate_format(:email, ~r/^[^\s]+@[^\s]+$/)
    |> Ecto.Changeset.apply_action(:validate)
  end

  def create(conn, params) do
    with {:ok, validated} <- validate_params(params) do
      render(conn, :show, user: validated)
    end
  end
end

bytes.topic: ORM & Database — query builders vs full ORMs, N+1 prevention, migrations
bytes.title: Phoenix & Ecto — Killing N+1s and Owning Your Migrations
bytes.body:
Ecto is Phoenix's data layer — a query builder and data mapper, not a traditional ORM. It never hides SQL behind magic; you compose queries explicitly using the `Ecto.Query` DSL and execute them through `Repo`.

The classic N+1 trap: calling `Repo.all(Post)` then looping to load associations individually. Ecto's fix is `Repo.preload/2`, which batches association loads into a single `IN` query. When you need to filter on the association itself, use a joined preload — combine `join: c in assoc(p, :comments)` with `preload: [comments: c]` inside a single `from/2` pipeline. This keeps it one round-trip and lets the database do the filtering.

Migrations live in `priv/repo/migrations/` and are generated via `mix ecto.gen.migration add_approved_to_comments`. Use `change/0` for reversible ops; fall back to explicit `up/0` and `down/0` when rollback semantics differ. Ecto tracks applied migrations in `schema_migrations` — always run `mix ecto.migrate` in CI before boot. Phoenix does not auto-migrate.

Changesets decouple validation and casting from persistence, keeping business rules explicit and unit-testable without a live database connection.
bytes.code_snippet:
# N+1 — avoid this
posts = Repo.all(Post)
Enum.map(posts, fn post -> Repo.preload(post, :comments) end)

# Batch preload — two queries total
posts = Post |> Repo.all() |> Repo.preload(:comments)

# Joined preload — one query, filter on association
from(p in Post,
  join: c in assoc(p, :comments),
  where: c.approved == true,
  preload: [comments: c]
)
|> Repo.all()

bytes.topic: Dependency Injection — built-in DI containers vs manual wiring, testability impact
bytes.title: Phoenix Dependency Injection — compile_env Wiring, Behaviours, and Mox Testability
bytes.body:
Phoenix has no IoC container. Dependencies are wired through Elixir's module system using `Application.compile_env/3` (compile-time) or `Application.get_env/3` (runtime). The pattern: define a `@behaviour` as your interface contract, configure the real implementation in `config/runtime.exs`, and swap it in tests without touching business logic.

`compile_env` bakes the module attribute at compile time — zero ETS lookups at runtime. Trade-off: changing the adapter requires recompilation. Use `get_env` inside the function body when you need hot-swappable adapters (e.g. payment gateways, feature-flagged providers).

Testability is built on `Mox`, which generates test doubles strictly against a behaviour contract. If your mock doesn't implement every callback, Mox raises at definition — not at test runtime. Crucially, Mox uses per-process expectations, making it safe for `async: true` tests.

The anti-pattern to avoid: calling `MyApp.RealMailer.deliver/3` directly inside business logic. That hardwires the dependency and forces you to either hit the real service or monkey-patch globally in tests. Always indirect through a compile_env-configured module attribute.

bytes.topic: Error Handling — global error handlers, consistent error response format
bytes.title: Phoenix Error Handling — Global Handlers and Consistent Error Responses
bytes.body:
Phoenix centralizes error handling through `Plug.ErrorHandler` and the `FallbackController` pattern. Rather than scattering error logic across controllers, define a fallback module and plug it in with `action_fallback MyAppWeb.FallbackController`. This controller handles `{:error, reason}` tuples returned from controller actions, keeping your happy path clean.

For consistent JSON error responses, implement `call/2` clauses pattern-matching on common error types: `Ecto.Changeset` for validation failures, `:not_found` for missing resources, and `:unauthorized` for auth failures. Each clause renders a structured response with a consistent shape — `%{errors: ..., status: ...}`.

For truly global errors (crashes, unhandled exceptions), override `render/2` in your `ErrorView` (or `ErrorHTML`/`ErrorJSON` in Phoenix 1.7+). The `errors.json` template catches unhandled cases and returns a sanitized message. Pair this with `Plug.ErrorHandler` in your `Endpoint` to intercept 404s and 500s before they hit the default behavior. This two-layer approach — FallbackController for domain errors, ErrorJSON for infrastructure errors — gives you full coverage without duplication.
bytes.code_snippet:
defmodule MyAppWeb.FallbackController do
  use MyAppWeb, :controller

  def call(conn, {:error, %Ecto.Changeset{} = changeset}) do
    conn
    |> put_status(:unprocessable_entity)
    |> put_view(json: MyAppWeb.ChangesetJSON)
    |> render(:error, changeset: changeset)
  end

  def call(conn, {:error, :not_found}) do
    conn
    |> put_status(:not_found)
    |> json(%{errors: %{detail: "Resource not found"}})
  end

  def call(conn, {:error, :unauthorized}) do
    conn
    |> put_status(:unauthorized)
    |> json(%{errors: %{detail: "Unauthorized"}})
  end
end

bytes.topic: Testing — unit vs integration, mocking HTTP, test database strategy
bytes.title: Phoenix Testing — Unit vs Integration, Mocking HTTP, and Sandbox Strategy
bytes.body:
Phoenix separates concerns cleanly via two generated test helpers: `DataCase` (Ecto sandbox, no HTTP) and `ConnCase` (full Plug pipeline + router). `Ecto.Adapters.SQL.Sandbox` wraps each test in a transaction that rolls back automatically, enabling `async: true` without data collisions — keep it on by default.

For HTTP clients, never call HTTPoison or Req directly from business logic. Define a behaviour module, implement it in production, and inject a `Mox`-backed mock in test env via `config :my_app, :http_client, MyApp.HTTPClientMock` in `config/test.exs`. This keeps unit tests fast and deterministic. Use `Bypass` when you need to assert on real HTTP wire format — it spins up a local Cowboy instance that captures outgoing requests.

Rule of thumb: unit-test pure context functions (`MyApp.Accounts.create_user/1`); integration-test controllers and LiveViews through `ConnCase` with `Phoenix.ConnTest` helpers like `get/2` and `json_response/2`. Avoid `Sandbox.mode(:shared)` except when external OS processes (e.g., Wallaby browser tests) need DB access. `verify_on_exit!/1` in Mox ensures no unexpected calls slip through.
bytes.code_snippet:
# lib/my_app/http_client.ex
defmodule MyApp.HTTPClient do
  @callback get(String.t()) :: {:ok, map()} | {:error, term()}
end

# test/support/mocks.ex
Mox.defmock(MyApp.HTTPClientMock, for: MyApp.HTTPClient)

# test/my_app/weather_test.exs
defmodule MyApp.WeatherTest do
  use MyApp.DataCase, async: true
  import Mox

  setup :verify_on_exit!

  test "fetches current temperature" do
    expect(MyApp.HTTPClientMock, :get, fn _url ->
      {:ok, %{"temp" => 72}}
    end)

    assert {:ok, %{temp: 72}} = MyApp.Weather.current("NYC")
  end
end

bytes.topic: Security — CORS, rate limiting, security headers, injection prevention
bytes.title: Phoenix Security — CORS, Rate Limiting, Headers, and Injection Prevention in One Pipeline
bytes.body:
Phoenix security is configured at the Plug pipeline level in `router.ex`, not scattered across controllers. For CORS, add `CORSPlug` with an explicit origin whitelist — wildcard origins in production are a misconfiguration, not a feature. Rate limiting fits naturally into the same pipeline via `Hammer.Plug`, using token-bucket semantics keyed by IP or user ID. `ex_rated` is a leaky-bucket alternative if you need smoother throttling.

Security headers (HSTS, CSP, X-Frame-Options, X-Content-Type-Options) are easiest to enforce with `plug_secex`, which composes into the pipeline and ships with hardened defaults. You can override per-header via options or fall back to raw `put_resp_header/3` in a custom plug.

Ecto's query DSL is parameterized by default — `from u in User, where: u.email == ^email` compiles to a `$1` binding in PostgreSQL, making SQL injection structurally impossible through the ORM. The only real injection surface is `Ecto.Adapters.SQL.query/3` with raw SQL: always pass values as the third-argument list, never interpolate into the query string.
bytes.code_snippet:
# router.ex
pipeline :api do
  plug :accepts, ["json"]
  plug CORSPlug,
    origin: ["https://yourapp.com"],
    methods: ["GET", "POST", "PUT", "DELETE"]
  plug PlugSecex
  plug Hammer.Plug,
    rate_limit: {"api_req", 60_000, 100},
    by: :ip
end

# Safe — Ecto parameterizes automatically via ^-pinning
from(u in User, where: u.email == ^email) |> Repo.one()

# Safe raw SQL — params as third argument, never interpolated
Ecto.Adapters.SQL.query(Repo, "SELECT * FROM users WHERE email = $1", [email])

bytes.topic: API Design — REST conventions, versioning strategy, OpenAPI generation
bytes.title: Phoenix API Design — Versioning with Scopes and OpenAPI via open_api_spex
bytes.body: Phoenix's router `scope` macro is the idiomatic way to version APIs. Nest a versioned scope inside a parent `/api` scope with `as:` aliases to avoid route helper collisions. Use the `resources/3` macro to generate RESTful routes — it enforces REST conventions and reduces boilerplate compared to hand-writing each `get`/`post`. For OpenAPI generation, `open_api_spex` integrates directly into controllers via `use OpenApiSpex.ControllerSpecs`. Declare `operation/2` above each action with typed request/response schemas. Add `plug OpenApiSpex.Plug.CastAndValidate` to your `:api` pipeline to get automatic parameter coercion and validation against your spec — no separate validation layer needed. Serve the spec itself at `/api/openapi` using `OpenApiSpex.Plug.RenderSpec`. This setup gives you contract-first design, runtime validation, and a living Swagger UI from a single source of truth. Keep v1 controllers in `lib/my_app_web/controllers/v1/` and v2 in `v2/` — Phoenix's module namespacing maps cleanly to folder structure.
bytes.code_snippet:
# router.ex
scope "/api", MyAppWeb do
  pipe_through [:api, :openapi_validate]

  scope "/v1", V1, as: :v1 do
    resources "/users", UserController, only: [:index, :show, :create]
  end

  get "/openapi", OpenApiSpex.Plug.RenderSpec, []
end

# controllers/v1/user_controller.ex
defmodule MyAppWeb.V1.UserController do
  use MyAppWeb, :controller
  use OpenApiSpex.ControllerSpecs

  operation :index,
    summary: "List users",
    responses: [ok: {"User list", "application/json", Schemas.UserListResponse}]

  def index(conn, _params) do
    render(conn, :index, users: Accounts.list_users())
  end
end

bytes.topic: Authentication — JWT validation, session handling, auth middleware patterns
bytes.title: Phoenix Authentication — JWT Pipelines with Guardian
bytes.body:
Phoenix authentication is built around Plug pipelines, not middleware classes. The idiomatic pattern uses Guardian to compose discrete plugs into reusable pipelines scoped per router scope.

A Guardian pipeline module chains `VerifyHeader`, `EnsureAuthenticated`, and `LoadResource` as plugs. Each plug either enriches `conn.assigns` or halts the connection with an error tuple — no exceptions, just data flow. Your router then pipes specific scopes through `:auth_required` while leaving public routes untouched.

For session-based flows, `Guardian.Plug.LoadResource` populates `conn.assigns.current_user` from the JWT subject claim, making it available downstream without any explicit lookup. Token refresh is handled via `Guardian.encode_and_sign/3` with a short `:ttl` and a separate refresh token stored in an HTTP-only cookie.

Avoid putting auth logic in controllers — keep it in pipeline plugs. This keeps controllers thin and makes auth behavior declarative in the router, where scope boundaries are immediately visible.
bytes.code_snippet:
defmodule MyAppWeb.AuthPipeline do
  use Guardian.Plug.Pipeline,
    otp_app: :my_app,
    module: MyApp.Guardian,
    error_handler: MyAppWeb.AuthErrorHandler

  plug Guardian.Plug.VerifyHeader, scheme: "Bearer"
  plug Guardian.Plug.EnsureAuthenticated
  plug Guardian.Plug.LoadResource, allow_blank: false
end

# router.ex
pipeline :api_auth do
  plug MyAppWeb.AuthPipeline
end

scope "/api", MyAppWeb do
  pipe_through [:api, :api_auth]
  resources "/posts", PostController
end

bytes.topic: Configuration — env vars, settings classes, secrets management
bytes.title: Phoenix Configuration — Runtime vs Compile-Time and Secrets Done Right
bytes.body:
Phoenix splits configuration into two distinct phases: compile-time (`config/config.exs`, `config/dev.exs`) and runtime (`config/runtime.exs`). The critical rule: all `System.get_env/1` calls for secrets and environment-specific values belong in `runtime.exs`, not `config.exs`. Reading env vars at compile time bakes values into the release binary, meaning your production Docker image would need to be rebuilt for every environment change.

In `runtime.exs`, use `System.fetch_env!/1` for required vars — it raises at startup if the var is missing, giving you a fast, explicit failure. For optional vars, use `System.get_env/2` with a default. Read config at runtime with `Application.fetch_env!/2` inside your modules.

For secrets in production releases, Phoenix 1.7+ generates `config/runtime.exs` with a `secret_key_base` block out of the box. Never hardcode this — run `mix phx.gen.secret` to generate a 64-byte value and inject it via `SECRET_KEY_BASE`. For database credentials, pass `DATABASE_URL` and let Ecto parse it via `Repo.config/0`.
bytes.code_snippet:
# config/runtime.exs
import Config

if config_env() == :prod do
  database_url =
    System.fetch_env!("DATABASE_URL")

  config :my_app, MyApp.Repo,
    url: database_url,
    pool_size: String.to_integer(System.get_env("POOL_SIZE", "10"))

  secret_key_base =
    System.fetch_env!("SECRET_KEY_BASE")

  config :my_app, MyAppWeb.Endpoint,
    secret_key_base: secret_key_base
end

bytes.topic: Performance — connection pooling, async handlers, response compression
bytes.title: Phoenix Performance — Connection Pooling, Async Assigns, and Bandit Compression
bytes.body: Phoenix's performance ceiling is largely determined by three knobs: Ecto pool pressure, async rendering, and HTTP compression. DBConnection's `queue_target` (default 50ms) and `queue_interval` (default 1000ms) control checkout behavior — if average queue wait exceeds `queue_target` over a rolling `queue_interval` window, the pool starts rejecting checkouts with `DBConnection.ConnectionError` to shed load early rather than cascade. Tune `pool_size` to roughly `(CPU cores × 2) + 1` as a starting point, then adjust based on `telemetry` queue measurements. For LiveView, `assign_async/3` (introduced in Phoenix.LiveView 0.20) offloads expensive data fetching off the mount path — the socket renders immediately with a loading state, then patches when the async result arrives, eliminating full render blocking. In regular controllers, wrap external calls in `Task.async/Task.await` with an explicit timeout to avoid holding a Cowboy/Bandit connection thread indefinitely. Compression is a one-liner with Bandit (the default HTTP server since Phoenix 1.7): set `compress: true` in your endpoint's `http` options. No separate Plug needed — Bandit negotiates gzip/deflate via `Accept-Encoding` automatically.
bytes.code_snippet:
# config/runtime.exs — Ecto pool tuning
config :my_app, MyApp.Repo,
  pool_size: 10,
  queue_target: 5_000,
  queue_interval: 50_000

# endpoint.ex — Bandit gzip (no extra plug needed)
plug MyAppWeb.Endpoint, http: [compress: true]

# LiveView — non-blocking async assign
def mount(_params, _session, socket) do
  {:ok,
   assign_async(socket, :feed, fn ->
     {:ok, %{feed: MyApp.Feed.load(socket.assigns.current_user_id)}}
   end)}
end

bytes.topic: Serialization — request/response DTOs, camelCase vs snake_case, null handling
bytes.title: Phoenix Serialization — DTOs, camelCase, and Null Handling in JSON Views
bytes.body:
Phoenix's JSON serialization chain runs: Controller → View module (`UserJSON`) → `Jason.encode!/1`. Ecto schema fields are snake_case internally, but REST clients often expect camelCase. Phoenix gives you zero automatic conversion — you own the mapping explicitly inside your `*_json.ex` view module.

The `*_json.ex` pattern (Phoenix 1.7+) replaces template files with plain Elixir functions returning plain maps, pattern-matched against controller assigns. This is your DTO layer: rename fields, strip sensitive keys, coerce types. Never use `@derive [Jason.Encoder]` on Ecto schemas — it leaks every field, including passwords and internal flags.

For null handling, `Jason` renders `nil` as JSON `null` automatically. But `null` and a missing key are semantically different for many clients. Omit the key entirely for "not applicable"; emit `null` for "known to be empty." Use conditional `Map.put/3` for optional fields rather than always including them.

For camelCase, manual key mapping in the view beats libraries like `ProperCase` — you get compile-time safety, explicit control, and no runtime overhead on every response.
bytes.code_snippet:
# lib/my_app_web/controllers/user_json.ex
defmodule MyAppWeb.UserJSON do
  def show(%{user: user}), do: %{data: data(user)}
  def index(%{users: users}), do: %{data: Enum.map(users, &data/1)}

  defp data(user) do
    base = %{
      id: user.id,
      fullName: user.full_name,
      avatarUrl: user.avatar_url,
      createdAt: user.inserted_at
    }
    # Omit :bio key entirely when nil; emit null when explicitly empty string
    if user.bio, do: Map.put(base, :bio, user.bio), else: base
  end
end

bytes.topic: Dev Tooling — hot reload, code generation, scaffolding CLI
bytes.title: Phoenix's Generator CLI — Scaffold Smarter with mix phx.gen
bytes.body: Phoenix's `mix phx.gen.*` commands do far more than stub files — they wire the full vertical slice. `mix phx.gen.live Catalog Product products name:string price:decimal` generates an Ecto schema, migration, context with CRUD functions, a LiveView module, and the router mount in one shot. The `--no-context` and `--no-schema` flags let you attach to an existing context or schema without clobbering it. For JSON APIs, `mix phx.gen.json` skips LiveView and produces controller + Ecto artifacts only. Auth gets its own generator: `mix phx.gen.auth Accounts User users` scaffolds token-based session auth complete with hashed passwords, confirmation emails, and LiveView login flows — nothing you'd want to hand-roll. Hot reload in Phoenix dev mode is handled by `phoenix_live_reload`; file patterns are configured in `config/dev.exs` under the `:live_reload` key. Adding a custom watcher (e.g., a Tailwind CLI process) is a single entry in the `watchers` list — Phoenix spawns and supervises it automatically alongside the server.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Generate a full LiveView CRUD feature
mix phx.gen.live Catalog Product products \
  name:string price:decimal stock:integer

# Generate JSON API resources (no LiveView)
mix phx.gen.json Accounts User users email:string:unique

# Scaffold token-based auth (hashing, sessions, confirmation)
mix phx.gen.auth Accounts User users

# config/dev.exs — add a Tailwind watcher
config :my_app, MyAppWeb.Endpoint,
  watchers: [
    tailwind: {Tailwind, :install_and_run, [:default, ~w(--watch)]}
  ]

bytes.topic: Logging — structured logs, correlation IDs, request logging middleware
bytes.title: Phoenix Logging — Structured Logs, Correlation IDs, and Request Middleware
bytes.body:
Phoenix ships with `Plug.RequestId` and `Plug.Logger` already wired in `endpoint.ex`, but default output is plain-text and loses correlation context across the plug pipeline. For production observability, you need a `request_id` threaded through every log line.

`Plug.RequestId` reads `X-Request-ID` from incoming headers or generates a UUID and stores it in `conn.assigns[:request_id]`. Place a custom plug immediately after it to call `Logger.metadata/1` — this writes into the process dictionary, so every `Logger.info/2` call downstream (including inside Contexts, LiveView mounts, and Ecto queries) automatically carries the correlation ID.

For JSON-structured output, add `logger_json` to your deps and point `config :logger, backends: [LoggerJSON]` in `config/prod.exs`. It serializes all metadata keys as discrete JSON fields — no format string interpolation, no grep-unfriendly blobs.

Avoid `Plug.Logger` under load. Prefer `:telemetry` events emitted by Phoenix itself — `[:phoenix, :endpoint, :stop]` gives you `duration`, `conn.status`, and the matched route as structured data, not a pre-formatted string you have to parse back out.
bytes.code_snippet:
# lib/my_app_web/plugs/request_logger.ex
defmodule MyAppWeb.Plugs.RequestLogger do
  import Plug.Conn
  require Logger

  def init(opts), do: opts

  def call(conn, _opts) do
    request_id = conn.assigns[:request_id] || Base.encode16(:crypto.strong_rand_bytes(8), case: :lower)
    Logger.metadata(
      request_id: request_id,
      method: conn.method,
      path: conn.request_path
    )
    conn
  end
end

bytes.topic: Background Jobs — task queues, scheduled jobs, worker process patterns
bytes.title: Phoenix Background Jobs — Oban Workers, Cron Scheduling, and Supervision Trees
bytes.body:
Oban is the de facto background job library in Phoenix, backed by PostgreSQL for durability — no Redis or separate broker needed. Define a worker with `use Oban.Worker, queue: :notifications, max_attempts: 3`, then implement `perform(%Oban.Job{args: args})` returning `:ok` or `{:error, reason}`. Insert jobs via `Oban.insert/2` with a plain args map.

Queues and concurrency are configured when Oban is added to your Application supervision tree. Each queue runs a pool of BEAM processes — `concurrency: 10` means 10 simultaneous workers per node, with queue isolation preventing a slow queue from starving others.

For cron scheduling, add a `:cron` list to Oban's config: `{"0 9 * * *", MyApp.Workers.DailyDigestWorker}`. Oban's scheduler runs in-process under the same supervisor — no external cron daemon or Quantum dependency required.

Retries use exponential backoff by default. Failed jobs land in a dead-letter state queryable via `Oban.Job` schema. For deduplication, add `unique: [period: 60, fields: [:args]]` to the worker — Oban inserts a DB-level unique constraint, preventing duplicate scheduling across nodes. For high-throughput event pipelines, Broadway over SQS/RabbitMQ is more appropriate; Oban excels at reliable, retryable, low-to-medium volume work.
bytes.code_snippet:
defmodule MyApp.Workers.NotificationWorker do
  use Oban.Worker, queue: :notifications, max_attempts: 3,
                   unique: [period: 300, fields: [:args]]

  @impl Oban.Worker
  def perform(%Oban.Job{args: %{"user_id" => user_id}}) do
    MyApp.Notifications.send_push(user_id)
    :ok
  end
end

# Enqueue a job
%{"user_id" => 42}
|> MyApp.Workers.NotificationWorker.new()
|> Oban.insert()

# In config/config.exs — cron scheduling, no external daemon
config :my_app, Oban, cron: [{"0 9 * * *", MyApp.Workers.DailyDigestWorker}]

bytes.topic: Framework-specific — Django batteries-included, FastAPI auto-docs, NestJS decorators, Phoenix LiveView
bytes.title: Phoenix LiveView — Stateful Server Components Without a Line of JavaScript
bytes.body:
Phoenix LiveView turns your server process into a stateful UI component. Each connected client maps to a GenServer-backed `LiveView` process holding its own `socket.assigns`. On `mount/3`, you initialize state; on `handle_event/3`, you mutate it — LiveView diffs the HEEx template and pushes only the changed HTML over a persistent WebSocket.

The critical insight: `assign/2` triggers a re-render of only the template subtrees that reference the changed key. Pair this with `Phoenix.LiveView.Helpers.stream/3` for large collections and you avoid full-list re-renders — LiveView tracks each item by `:id` and emits surgical DOM patches.

For navigation, `push_patch/2` updates URL params and re-invokes `handle_params/3` without a full remount, keeping socket state intact. `push_navigate/2` triggers a full remount — use it only when crossing LiveView boundaries.

Avoid blocking `handle_event/3` with slow I/O. Instead, spawn a `Task.async` and receive the result in `handle_info/2` to keep the socket responsive. LiveView's per-client process model makes this natural — no shared mutable state, no locks.
bytes.code_snippet:
defmodule MyAppWeb.SearchLive do
  use MyAppWeb, :live_view

  def mount(_params, _session, socket) do
    {:ok, assign(socket, query: "", results: [])}
  end

  def handle_event("search", %{"q" => q}, socket) do
    task = Task.async(fn -> MyApp.Search.run(q) end)
    {:noreply, assign(socket, query: q, task: task)}
  end

  def handle_info({ref, results}, socket) when socket.assigns.task.ref == ref do
    Process.demonitor(ref, [:flush])
    {:noreply, assign(socket, results: results, task: nil)}
  end
end

bytes.topic: Monitoring — health check endpoints, metrics exposure, distributed tracing
bytes.title: Phoenix Monitoring — Health Checks, PromEx Metrics, and OpenTelemetry Tracing
bytes.body:
Phoenix ships with first-class telemetry hooks via `:telemetry` events emitted by `Plug.Telemetry`, `Phoenix.Router`, and `Ecto`. Build a structured health endpoint by routing `GET /health` to a controller that pings Repo and any critical dependencies, returning 200 or 503 based on check results.

For Prometheus metrics, `PromEx` is the idiomatic choice — it wraps `TelemetryMetrics` and auto-instruments Phoenix, Ecto, Oban, and Beam VM stats. Drop `MyApp.PromEx` into your supervision tree, add the `/metrics` plug to your router, and Grafana dashboards work out of the box using the bundled pre-built panels.

For distributed tracing, add `opentelemetry_phoenix` and `opentelemetry_ecto` to your deps. Both attach to existing `:telemetry` events — no manual instrumentation needed. Configure the OTLP exporter in `config/runtime.exs` pointing at your Jaeger or Honeycomb collector. Spans are automatically named after your Phoenix controller actions and Ecto query sources, giving you end-to-end traces across service boundaries with zero boilerplate.
bytes.code_snippet:
# router.ex
get "/health", HealthController, :show
Plug.Metrics |> forward "/metrics", to: PromEx.Plug, prom_ex_module: MyApp.PromEx

# health_controller.ex
def show(conn, _params) do
  checks = %{db: db_reachable?(), cache: cache_reachable?()}
  status = if Enum.all?(Map.values(checks), & &1), do: :ok, else: :service_unavailable
  conn |> put_status(status) |> json(checks)
end

# application.ex
children = [MyApp.Repo, MyApp.PromEx, MyAppWeb.Endpoint]

# config/runtime.exs
config :opentelemetry, :processors,
  otel_batch_processor: %{exporter: {:opentelemetry_exporter, %{endpoints: [System.get_env("OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT")]}}}
